-FROM THE PROVINCES

Leix and Ossory

At last we have got the Training Fund
Committee in working order, and the neces-
+ sary circulars and collecting cards are being
distributed amongst the. various clubs
throughout the county. Te will take atleast
£50.0r £60 to get our men through a
proper system of training, and if the re-
sponse is anything like what it should be
the committee will be able to put a team in
the field when the day arrives that will do
honour to Leix. I would earnestly appeal
toall the Gaels of Leix and Ossory, and
those living in other counties, to subscribe
towards this fund, as ic isthe first time that
cour players have called upon the rank and
file to do their part. It is necessary to state
the players thoughout the Leinster cham-
pionship bore the expenses of training them-
selves 5 and would it be fair, I ask, to have
them bear even greater expense to train
for an All-Ireland final? Nearly all the
players are working men, and their appeal
to the people is being watched with confi-
dence, ‘Thete are 1,200 shillings in £¢
and it would not be’ difficult to get that
number of subscribers to make it up. For
the information of those willing to sub-
stribe I give the names of those to whom
subscriptions can be sent :—=Rev. J. J.
Kearney, C.C, President, Maryboro’ ;
Mr, James Miller, trustee, Mountrath ;
Messis, J. J. Higgins and J. Sheridan, hon.
secs. Training Fund, Maryboro’.

The final of the Senior football cham-
pionship is fixed for Portleix on Sunday,
September 27th, between The Heath and
Ballyroan,

Our Junior county.team is to play Meath
in the ‘semicfinal of the Leinster hurling
championship at Croke Park on Sunday,
October t2th.

‘At meeting of South’ Wales County

in view of complications likely to arise that
the bye-laws governing the constitution of

the Board be allowed to remain in force
for this yeat. A letter was also read from
the Irish National Club and Institute,
Tonypandy, re the formation of new

hurling club and requesting particulars
Mr. P. Cody, Tonypandy, pointed out that
the formation of the new club would
seriously interfere with the existing club
and thought there was not room for the
two clubs in the disteict. The matter was
fully discussed and the meeting, though
anxious to welcome new clubs and assist,
considered that in this case it would be
better if the present Tonypandy club were
supported by intending members of the new
club, and the secretary was instructed to
communicate with the Irish National club

accordingly. The design for the cham-
pionship medals was approved and the
